slides.heading: Where does your stuff actually live?
slides.body:
Think about when you take a photo on your phone.

You close the camera app. You turn the phone off. You come back an hour later — and the photo is still there.

How? Where did it go when the app was closed?

Your stuff is saved — stored — in a physical place inside your device. It's always there, even when the screen is off.
bullets: 📸 Take a photo — it gets saved
📱 Close the app — the photo is still there
🔋 Turn off the phone — nothing is lost
💾 Storage is the place that keeps your stuff safe, permanently

slides.heading: Storage is like a bookshelf — it keeps things long-term
slides.body:
Imagine you're studying. You pick up a book from your shelf and open it on your desk.

Your desk holds what you're actively working with — it's quick to grab, but small in space.

Your bookshelf holds everything else — all your books, notes, photos. It's slower to grab from, but it never forgets anything.

Computers work the same way. We're going to focus on the bookshelf — the part that saves your things permanently.
bullets: 🖥️ Desk = the workspace for what you're using right now
📖 Opening an app = taking a book off the shelf
💾 Saving a file = putting a note back on the shelf

slides.heading: Folders and files — you already know this
slides.body:
You've seen folders before — on a computer, on your phone, maybe in a real filing cabinet.

A folder is just a way to group related things together. And inside a folder, you have individual pieces — documents, photos, music files.

Computers organize storage the exact same way. Everything lives in folders, inside other folders, in a big organized tree.

When you learn Linux, you'll be navigating this tree — moving between folders, listing what's inside them, creating new ones.
bullets: 📄 File = one piece of stuff (a photo, a song, a document)
🌳 Storage is organized like a tree of folders
👉 Next: you'll explore this tree yourself

slides.heading: On Linux, you have your own personal folder — your home
slides.body:
When you log into a Linux system, you land in a special place called your home folder.

Think of it as your own room in a shared apartment. Other people might use the same computer, but your home folder is yours — your files, your settings, your space.

In the next steps, you'll open the terminal and start exploring. You'll see exactly where you are, what's around you, and how to move through this space.
bullets: 🏠 Home folder = your personal space on the computer
🔑 Other users can't touch your files
🗺️ The terminal is how you explore this space
✅ You're almost ready to try it yourself!
